topic: Las Vegas Case Study: Monsoon Water Intrusion Repair for Stucco Staining, Soft Trim, and Interior Wall Damage
excerpt: In Las Vegas, that pattern makes sense. The local Las Vegas soil series is shallow, well-drained, and carbonatic/caliche-heavy, so heavy rain does not always soak in gently; it can run fast, splash hard, and concentrate water at slab edges and low walls. Thunderstorms and monsoon bursts in southern Nevada can produce flash flooding and damaging winds, which is exactly when small exterior weak points stop being “cosmetic” and start becoming real leak paths. On this kind of project, the homeowner symptoms are usually obvious after the storm: fresh stucco staining, damp garage or lower-wall drywall, swollen trim, and a musty corner that was not there before. The durable repair logic is straight physics—stop the entry point, improve the discharge path, remove what stayed wet too long, then rebuild and seal the assembly so the next storm does not reopen the whole mess.
markdown_tables:
| Case study snapshot | What was going on | How the scope was handled |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Gutter/discharge corner | Water dumping too close to wall and slab edge | Reworked discharge and corrected flow path |
| Lower stucco / trim zone | Splash-back staining and soft exterior trim | Removed failed sections, rebuilt, sealed all faces |
| Interior wall damage | Bubbling paint, soft drywall, visible stain line | Cut out compromised material, dried area, rebuilt finish |
| Door / opening nearby | Slight latch drift after repeated wetting/movement | Tuned hinges/strike and corrected reveal |
| Finish quality issue | Prior patching visible in strong hallway light | Wide-float blend and prime-ready finish cleanup |
| Project size | Multiple connected failures, not a one-trade patch | Scoped as one high-ticket water-intrusion package |
